name: Fergus mac Roich
description: Fergus mac Róich, a central figure in the Ulster Cycle of Irish mythology, is renowned for his immense size, strength, and legendary prowess as a warrior. He was once the King of Ulster, but relinquished the throne to Conchobar mac Nessa after being tricked. Despite this betrayal, Fergus remained loyal to Ulster, serving as a protector and champion. He is depicted as a generous, honorable, and sometimes impulsive leader, known for his powerful sword Caladbolg and his insatiable appetite. His relationship with Queen Medb of Connacht is a complex one, often portrayed as an alliance of convenience and mutual respect, though they are on opposing sides in the Táin Bó Cúailnge (Cattle Raid of Cooley). Fergus's tragic flaw is his willingness to be swayed by oaths and obligations, even when they conflict with his own sense of justice. He ultimately meets his end due to treachery, a testament to the dangers inherent in a world of shifting alliances and broken promises. He embodies the heroic ideal of the Ulster Cycle, showcasing both extraordinary strength and vulnerability. His knowledge of ancient lore and his role as a foster father also contribute to his significance as a wise and influential figure.
subtitle: The Mighty Warrior and Former King
achievements: Fought alongside Medb in the Táin Bó Cúailnge

keyMyths.title: The Loss of the Kingship
keyMyths.description: Fergus was tricked into relinquishing the kingship of Ulster to Conchobar mac Nessa. This betrayal sets the stage for many of Fergus's later actions and alliances.
keyMyths.source: Various versions of the Ulster Cycle

keyMyths.title: The Táin Bó Cúailnge
keyMyths.description: Fergus fights on the side of Connacht, led by Medb, in the Cattle Raid of Cooley against Ulster. His intimate knowledge of Ulster proves strategically valuable to Medb's forces.

keyMyths.title: Fergus's Death
keyMyths.description: Fergus is ultimately killed due to treachery and the breaking of oaths. His death highlights the dangers of honor and loyalty in a world of political intrigue.
keyMyths.source: Various versions of the Ulster Cycle

sources.author: Thomas Kinsella
sources.description: A modern translation of the Táin Bó Cúailnge, a central text for understanding Fergus's role.
sources.text: A modern translation of the Táin Bó Cúailnge, a central text for understanding Fergus's role.

sources.author: Jeffrey Gantz
sources.description: Provides a comprehensive overview of the Ulster Cycle and Fergus's place within it.
